tweets.text:
From the Markets Update (https://t.co/URqvQfM9WQ):

Despite another (small) SPX gain, for a second session we had just 2 of 11 sectors higher according to CME Cash Indices, but again led by the heavyweight Tech sector +2.5% after +1.9% Friday (and +20% in May), again keeping the index afloat. The other was lightweight Energy +2.9%, only its 2nd gain in 8 sessions.

Six sectors though down around -1% more (after five Friday) led by Utilities -3%. Consumer Discretionary was -2.6%, and RE, Comm Services, Staples, and Health Care were also down more than -1% (all down at least -0.9% Friday).

tweets.text:
China's crude oil inventory adjusted for imbalance.

Satellite data captures 50% of onshore inventories. Zero chance the data is what it is. They also have guardrails in place to prevent errenous data like a massive 30 million bbl decrease.

People taking satellite data at face value will see that it is revised retroactively and without warning.
tweets.full_text:
China's crude oil inventory adjusted for imbalance.

Satellite data captures 50% of onshore inventories. Zero chance the data is what it is. They also have guardrails in place to prevent errenous data like a massive 30 million bbl decrease.

People taking satellite data at face value will see that it is revised retroactively and without warning.

tweets.text:
The commentary from the ISM report didn't reflect the strong metrics: 

“25 percent of the comments were positive and 69 percent negative, with a 1-to-2.7 ratio of positive to negative sentiment. Among comments, the Iran war was mentioned in 42 percent and tariffs in 18 percent; 57 percent of the panelists mentioned pricing volatility as an issue.” 

Every single comment referenced higher prices but one (Transportation) which referenced supply chain constraints. In that regard a notable comment was from electrical equipment noting memory chip volatility, “increased gas prices and tarris are causing long lead constraints and price hikes that customers are not willing to bear. Panic is starting within our industry.”

A couple of mentions of stronger than expected demand but also a couple of weakening, but the overwhelming focus of the comments was prices and supply chains.

https://t.co/3YD6RO3FZM

From the Week Ahead (https://t.co/dK8IKSbarp):

Looking at potential vol control buying/selling this week, things are more favorable, although still just one 1%+ day falling off the lookbacks (still more than last week). 

The average for the rest of the days though on the 3-month lookback (the “trigger” given 1-month realized volatility remains under 3-month), is 0.78% up from 0.45% the prior week and 0.48% on the 1-month (from 0.2% the prior week).

tweets.text:
The clock is ticking. The moment China blinks, it's all over.

China is not going to balance the world on its own. Those hefty US exports are about to fall off a cliff in July.

China would need to reduce its crude imports to 3 million b/d, -8 million b/d y-o-y, or -240 million bbls a month to balance the global crude market.

Good fucking luck, especially now that we are going into peak summer demand months.

Every physical oil trader on the planet is watching every transaction like a hawk. The moment China steps in, they are going to frontrun it like no tomorrow.

China tried to get cute, now it's going to get fucked. Communists, thinking they can outsmart the invisible hand, inevitably get destroyed. 

It is what it is.

tweets.text:
Goldman notes the drop in 10yr Treasury yields has mostly been through term premium (the extra risk for carrying duration vs overnight rates) but says that may have run its course:

The long-end yield move has largely shown up as a compression in Treasury term premium, with only modest contribution from the expectations component of yields (Exhibit 1).

 That dynamic that has seen the yield curve move steadily flatter through the course of the month despite the back and forth in outright yield levels. While hawkish Fed communication has reinforced the curve shift, we think that the argument for continued long-end outperformance looks more challenging... We think a meaningful extension of the yield move would likely need to incorporate some shift in the distribution of risks around the Fed path.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
From the Week Ahead (https://t.co/SQmufwV7jr):

10yr term premium (the extra risk compensation for carrying duration (length) in a bond)) as of the week of May 11th (it’s reported with a little lag) jumped to the highest since 2011 (it likely though softened off that some last https://t.co/SKdC4o0PFJ
tweets.full_text:
Goldman notes the drop in 10yr Treasury yields has mostly been through term premium (the extra risk for carrying duration vs overnight rates) but says that may have run its course:

The long-end yield move has largely shown up as a compression in Treasury term premium, with only modest contribution from the expectations component of yields (Exhibit 1).

 That dynamic that has seen the yield curve move steadily flatter through the course of the month despite the back and forth in outright yield levels. While hawkish Fed communication has reinforced the curve shift, we think that the argument for continued long-end outperformance looks more challenging... We think a meaningful extension of the yield move would likely need to incorporate some shift in the distribution of risks around the Fed path.

tweets.text:
The % of manufacturing GDP that contracted fell sharply to just 2% (from 19% in April and 16% in March), while the share in strong contraction (PMI at or below 45, “a good barometer of overall manufacturing weakness”) also remained at 2% (same as April, down from 4% in March and well below 43% in Dec). 

16 of 18 industries overall expanded (up from 13 in Apr, same as March) led by printing, textile mills and nonmetallic minerals, while just one contracted (Wood Products).

But prices remained above 80 for a second month for the first time since April 2022. For a third straight month zero industries reported declining prices (16 reported higher prices), and no commodities appeared on the down list.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
ISM US Manufacturing PMI - May 2026
ISM manufacturing PMI accelerates to 4-year high with improving production, new orders, and employment with just 2% of manufacturing GDP contracting, but prices remain 2nd highest since 2022.

https://t.co/CajtWGUnSV https://t.co/XOYYh8bMKX
tweets.full_text:
The % of manufacturing GDP that contracted fell sharply to just 2% (from 19% in April and 16% in March), while the share in strong contraction (PMI at or below 45, “a good barometer of overall manufacturing weakness”) also remained at 2% (same as April, down from 4% in March and well below 43% in Dec). 

16 of 18 industries overall expanded (up from 13 in Apr, same as March) led by printing, textile mills and nonmetallic minerals, while just one contracted (Wood Products).

But prices remained above 80 for a second month for the first time since April 2022. For a third straight month zero industries reported declining prices (16 reported higher prices), and no commodities appeared on the down list.
